answer:
**Ghalib's maqtas (closing couplets/tak hallus shers) appear consistently at the end of his ghazals, often with a self-reflexive or ironic turn invoking his pen name.** Here are representative examples from *Diwan-e-Ghalib*:

- From ghazal 1 (*Aa ke meri jaan ko qarar nahin hai*):  
  You've sworn an oath, O Ghalib, to give up the wine,  
  But in your vow, the faith of any prayer is not there.[^1]

- From ghazal 6 (*Aamad-e-khat se hua hai sard jo bazaar-e-dost*):  
  I love this ghazal, Ghalib, from my very soul, Beloved,  
  Forcing my verse to echo with your name so much, Beloved.[^2]

- From ghazal 26 (*Dard minnat-kash-e-dawa na hua*):  
  "Do speak some verse," the people have begun to say,  
  "For Ghalib has not sung a single ghazal today."[^3]

- From ghazal 195 (*Waan pahunch kar jo ghash aata paye_ham hai hum ko*):  
  A single hope, O Ghalib, leads me onward past despair;  
  The road I walk, drawn by the hook of Grace's holy name, is the state I'm in.[^4]

A similar pattern holds in Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's *Diwan-e-Zauq*, his contemporary and rival:  
- From ghazal 26 (*Kal gaye the tum jise bimaar-e-hijraan chhod kar*):  
  Recite a fiery ghazal, Zauq, don't refrain, left behind,  
  The fire-souled lover’s style, don't let it wane, left behind.[^5]

**No Persian ghazals or maqtas are available in the Falsafa corpus** (0 Persian works; searches for "maqta," "takhallus," or "persian ghazal" surface only Urdu contexts). The corpus cannot address Urdu-Persian divergences in takhallus placement (typically penultimate matla/radeef-bound in both, but flexible), closing turn (often aphoristic/self-deprecating in Urdu), or rhetorical posture (Urdu more vernacular-personalized vs. Persian's courtly abstraction)—these are absent from the texts here.

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 1 (*Aa ke meri jaan ko qarar nahin hai*), [p-4304e1](https://falsafa.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/01-aa-ke-meri-jaan-ko-qarar-nahin-hai/translation/#p-4304e1).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 6 (*Aamad-e-khat se hua hai sard jo bazaar-e-dost*), [p-2705d0](https://falsafa.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/#p-2705d0).

[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 26 (*Dard minnat-kash-e-dawa na hua*), [p-57b20e](https://falsafa.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/#p-57b20e).

[^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 195 (*Waan pahunch kar jo ghash aata paye_ham hai hum ko*), [p-30dc4d](https://falsafa.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/195-waan-pahunch-kar-jo-ghash-aata-paye-ham-hai-hum-ko/translation/#p-30dc4d).

[^5]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 26 (*Kal gaye the tum jise bimaar-e-hijraan chhod kar*), [p-20030a](https://falsafa.com/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/26-kal-gaye-the-tum-jise-bimaar-e-hijraan-chhod-kar/translation/#p-20030a).
